Author: tholin
Content: So, about the fab SRAM macros: they have their VDD/VSS power straps on the Metal3 layer in the form of horizontal strips. However, the vertical Metal4 strips in chip_top’s power grid will not connect to them. Why?

Author: tholin
Content: Also, the SRAM macros have DRC errors.

Author: 246tnt
Content: Are you sure they do ?  AFAIR Magic thinks they do because the tech file doesn't know about sram rules. But if you checks they are all false positive

Author: rebelmike
Content: I believe the SRAM macro in https://github.com/MichaelBell/ttgf0p2-tinyQV/actions/runs/18697252507 is connected correctly, though would appreciate a double check on that!

Author: rebelmike
Content: However, that project is a bit stalled, both because of the DRC errors (which should be ignorable), and because when I increased the utilisation I started getting LVS errors.

Author: 246tnt
Content: It's connected through power but only on the end. The only reason I can think of for that is that the LEF only defines the end zone as pins for connections.

Author: 246tnt
Content: It's also connected in the center control zone. So maybe they wanted to avoid random vias in the SRAM core zone, maybe it can distord metal a bit or cause some stress that they didn't want to risk given the tighter rules they use in there.

Author: rebelmike
Content: Yeah, I had to be fairly precise aligning the met4 stripes to get the connections to align with the pins.

Author: tholin
Content: Any reason why only those end zones are valid pins? Could the LEF be updated to label the whole horizontal strips?

Author: 246tnt
Content: As stated above, the only reason I can think of is avoid any disturbance in the sram core area ...

Author: rzioma
Content:
Need advice! I would like to add 16KB of RAM (32 macro x SRAM 512 5V), I have quite a lot of free space, so it should fit. Any gotchas for laying out 32 blocks in a good manner for memory?

*(Z80 design I am working on has a lot of free space, I want to put 16KB of RAM. That particular combinations Z80+16KB RAM is useful for fixing DRAM in old computers)*

Author: _luke_w_
Content: If the bus was wider than 8 bits I'd say keep all the RAMs for the same byte lane in the same row or column, but that doesn't really apply here 😅

Author: _luke_w_
Content: Make sure to leave enough gap in between the RAMs for the tools to insert buffers. Generally pick one pair of orientations (N/S only or W/E only) so that fly-by routing over the top of the RAMs on M4/M5 does a better job of hooking up the address bus. It can be useful to alternate N-S-N-S because iirc the D connections are all on one side of the RAM, and the Q are all on the other side

Author: _luke_w_
Content: oh, and try to leave space in the middle of your RAM array for your design, so it's routing across half the chip (worst case) not the whole chip

Author: mithro_
Content: Fixing DRAM in old computers? Do you mean emulating DRAM using SRAM or something else?

Author: rzioma
Content:
Yes. The fix is to use onchip SRAM block connected directly to Z80 instead of external DRAM (which usually were multiple of ICs with high chance of failure during the long lifetime). External DRAM ICs are simply pulled from the mainboard of the computer.

Adding 64KB would be better and more generic, but I can't fit them right now.

Author: rzioma
Content: 16KB is good enough to fix that common breakage in ZX Spectrum family (allegedly the most sold Z80 home computer). They had 16KB (in 8ICs) of slightly faster memory which would die more often.

Author: rzioma
Content: @Tim 'mithro' Ansell I am also adding a couple of companion chips that were common in combination with Z80.

Author: rzioma
Content: Not yet sure it is worth the additional risk though 🙂

Author: rzioma
Content: Thank you!

Author: 246tnt
Content: Huh ... so as part of testing I wanted to remove the `DualGate` layer from the standar cells to make the transistors 3v3 instead of 5V. Yes, they're gate would be longer than needed because I'm not resizing them, just changing the oxide thickness, hoping for them to get faster basically.

Author: 246tnt
Content: But ... simulation shows they get _slower_ ....

Author: 246tnt
Content: First theory is of course, thinner oxide means more gate capacitance and whatever gain we have from the thinner oxide forming better channel gets overwhelmed by the higher gate capacitance.

Author: 246tnt
Content: I could also have screwed up my simulation somewhere ...

Author: tholin
Content: I'd say its because the gate lengths are still the same

Author: 246tnt
Content: Well yeah, I know it's because their length is the same, but through what effect.

Author: 246tnt
Content: At 3.3V Vg and Vds, Ids is higher for 3v3 one (as expected) so it's better at passing current. (same W/L)

Author: 246tnt
Content: Although looking at the curve, it's really not _that_ much better.

Author: bailey8889
Content: @tnt For a given gate size, wouldn't lower voltages mean slower circuits?

Author: 246tnt
Content: @bailey I'm changing the transistor type, but both get powered using 3v3 .

Author: 246tnt
Content: Of course if I'm powering the 5V with 5V they go faster 😁
